factual-accuracy.requirement: States HCI timing guidelines approximately 0.1s instantaneous, 1s preserves thought, beyond 10s loses attention
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the canonical HCI timing triad as 0.1 seconds (instantaneous), 1 second (preserves thought/mostly uninterrupted), and 10 seconds (beyond which users lose attention).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States inline suggestions should target 150-300ms first render to preserve flow state
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends a “Latency Target: 150–300ms” for inline code suggestions and states this “first render” window is crucial to preserve uninterrupted developer flow/flow state.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States multi-line suggestions acceptable at 300-1000ms with boundaries and visual affordances
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that multi-line/block completions are tolerable in the 300–750ms range (up to 1s) and emphasizes boundaries like a 150–300ms “first render” plus visual affordances such as immediate progress feedback/spinner/ghost token to manage perceived delay.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States comment-to-code generation tolerable at 0.5-2 seconds with streaming or progress indicators
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that for on-demand/comment-to-code generation (including CodeWhisperer), latencies up to 1 second are tolerated and that multi-line/comment-to-code can take 1–2 seconds if progress feedback appears quickly, which matches the 0.5–2 second tolerable range with streaming/progress indicators.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends streaming, progress indicators, or on-demand mode when generation exceeds timing budgets
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends immediate feedback mechanisms like spinners/progress indicators for slower multi-line completions and explicitly discusses using on-demand/manual suggestion requests for larger generations when latency is higher.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends on-demand or delayed triggers for debugging and proactive acceptable for feature development
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends proactive suggestions for new feature development (“Be proactive for new features/boilerplate”) and reduced proactivity for debugging, implying a more on-demand approach (“less so for debugging”). This explicitly supports using delayed/on-demand triggers for debugging while allowing proactive behavior for feature work.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States debugging has shorter edit-run cycles (approximately 1 minute) than feature development (approximately 3 minutes)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses debugging having lower latency tolerance and mentions feature work edit–run cycles in minutes/seconds, but it never states that debugging has ~1-minute edit-run cycles versus feature development at ~3 minutes. The specific approximate 1-minute vs 3-minute comparison is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States less-experienced developers show higher AI suggestion acceptance rates than 10+ year developers
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts experience levels, stating that “2–5 years” developers have “higher acceptance” and that “10+ years” developers “reject… more frequently,” which implies less-experienced developers accept AI suggestions at higher rates than 10+ year developers.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares GitHub Copilot inline suggestions, Tabnine multi-line predictions, and CodeWhisperer comment-to-code
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts GitHub Copilot’s inline suggestions, Tabnine’s multi-line predictions, and Amazon CodeWhisperer’s comment-to-code generation in a comparison table and surrounding discussion.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports acceptance rates 25-35% in production deployment
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports acceptance rates in the 25–35% range, including “Amazon CodeWhisperer … 27–31%” and other figures like “GitHub Copilot … 30–38%.” This includes values within 25–35% for production/field usage metrics.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports Copilot 10-15% acceptance gain OR CodeWhisperer 50-60% faster OR JetBrains 40-50% acceptance increase
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports a JetBrains-related acceptance change, stating “Acceptance up by 12% post-latency optimizations” for Copilot, but it does not report Copilot having a 10–15% acceptance gain, CodeWhisperer being 50–60% faster, or JetBrains having a 40–50% acceptance increase.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies trade-off between acceptance-rate optimization and code quality or retention metrics
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts maximizing acceptance rates with negative impacts on code quality/productivity, noting experienced developers can be “19% slower” due to review cost and irrelevant output and that latency optimizations raise acceptance. It also discusses how interruptions affect errors/bugs, implying a trade-off between pushing proactive suggestions for acceptance and preserving quality/retention outcomes.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Synthesizes conditional decision framework specifying when proactive versus on-demand presentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response synthesizes a decision framework by recommending proactive suggestions for new feature/boilerplate work and more on-demand/explicit suggestions for debugging and senior developers, tying these choices to latency thresholds and interruption sensitivity. It also adds conditional guidance (e.g., multi-line suggestions acceptable if feedback appears within 150–300ms).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Extracts vendor-specific deployment techniques with measured outcomes (e.g., filter +50% acceptance)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes vendor-specific deployment details with measured outcomes, such as GitHub Copilot acceptance increasing from 30% to 38% and “acceptance up by 12% post-latency optimizations,” along with tool-specific latency ranges and acceptance rates for Tabnine and CodeWhisperer.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides experience-level differentiated strategies with specific recommendations per cohort
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response differentiates recommendations by experience level (2–5 years vs 10+ years) and by task type (debugging vs new feature development), and it gives specific timing/latency targets and UI behaviors (proactive vs on-demand, first-render thresholds, explanation verbosity). These constitute cohort-specific strategies with concrete recommendations.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies escalation paths when timing budgets exceeded (streaming, progress indicators, on-demand)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides latency budgets and mentions using progress indicators/spinners for slower multi-line completions, but it does not specify explicit escalation paths or fallback behaviors when those timing budgets are exceeded (e.g., switching to streaming, degrading to progress-only, or forcing on-demand mode). It lacks a clear “if latency > X then do Y” escalation policy across streaming/progress/on-demand.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes appropriate trust calibration from blind acceptance with UI implementation patterns
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response contrasts trust calibration (adaptive, optional explanations, provenance, and compliance signals) with risks of distraction and over-trust, and it proposes concrete UI patterns like proactive vs on-demand suggestions, spinners/ghost tokens, and suppressing likely-rejected suggestions to avoid blind acceptance.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Acknowledges evidence gaps and recommends instrumentation for uncertain areas
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes uncertainty and variability in latency/acceptance findings and recommends instrumentation such as monitoring live latency and acceptance telemetry, tuning adaptively by context, and using behavioral data to suppress likely-rejected suggestions.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recommends proactive suggestions as universally beneficial without acknowledging flow state disruption or interruption cost research
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly emphasizes interruption costs and flow disruption (e.g., “risk of flow interruption,” “10–15 minutes to resume,” “strictly target 150–300ms”) and recommends proactive suggestions only conditionally (e.g., “Be proactive for new features… less so for debugging”). It does not present proactive suggestions as universally beneficial without acknowledging interruption research.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise timing terminology (latency thresholds, render time, flow state) with explicit millisecond or second units
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly uses precise timing terminology and includes explicit units such as 100ms, 150–300ms, 300–750ms, 1 second (1000ms), and 10 seconds (10,000ms), tying them to render/first-token timing and flow disruption thresholds.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes findings by tool (Copilot, Tabnine, CodeWhisperer) and cross-cuts by experience levels and task types
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes a tool-by-tool comparison section (GitHub Copilot, Tabnine, Amazon CodeWhisperer) and discusses differences by task type (debugging vs new feature development) and experience level (2–5 years vs 10+ years). These cross-cuts are integrated throughout the timing, acceptance, and trust/explanation discussions.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective research synthesis tone without marketing language or anecdotal personal experience
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, research-synthesis style throughout, focusing on latency thresholds, acceptance rates, and cited sources. It does not include marketing-style promotional language or anecdotal personal experience from the author.

presentation-quality.requirement: Concludes key sections with specific design recommendations derived from the research evidence
presentation-quality.explanation: The response ends with a “Summary of Actionable Best Practices for Enterprise Design” section that provides specific interface design recommendations (e.g., 150–300ms first-render target, feedback within 150–300ms, proactive vs on-demand by task/experience) explicitly tied to the preceding research evidence.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents comparisons as prose paragraphs rather than using tables, parallel sections, or labeled side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple tables (e.g., the vendor latency/UX breakdown and the latency vs. acceptance table) and clearly labeled sections with headings rather than presenting comparisons only as prose paragraphs.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites academic research on programmer interruption costs or AI code completion from arXiv, ACM, IEEE, or ResearchGate
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites academic research sources relevant to AI code completion and latency, including an arXiv paper (“Multi-line AI-assisted Code Authoring – Meta CodeCompose”) and a ResearchGate publication (“Are 100 ms Fast Enough?”), satisfying the requirement for academic citations from arXiv/ACM/IEEE/ResearchGate.

citation-quality.requirement: References JetBrains AI assistant deployment metrics or timing research from blog.jetbrains.com or official JetBrains publications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple JetBrains official publications from blog.jetbrains.com/research (e.g., “JetBrains Developer Ecosystem Survey 2025” and “JetBrains Research: Understanding AI's Impact on Developer Workflows”), thereby referencing JetBrains publications as requested.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites GitHub Copilot or Microsoft productivity studies from github.blog, microsoft.com/research, or ACM publications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites GitHub Copilot research from github.blog (e.g., “Building a faster, smarter GitHub Copilot…”), includes an ACM publication link (CACM article on Copilot productivity), and references Microsoft Research–hosted material (erichorvitz.com Copilot display paper associated with Microsoft Research).

citation-quality.requirement: References established HCI timing thresholds (Nielsen, speedcurve.com, or equivalent authoritative HCI sources)
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites established HCI timing thresholds, including Jakob Nielsen’s “0.1s, 1s, 10s” response-time guidelines, and references other authoritative HCI sources (e.g., Card, Moran & Newell; Shneiderman; Miller).

citation-quality.requirement: Includes research or metrics from at least 2 of AWS CodeWhisperer, Google Research, Tabnine, or other code completion vendors
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes vendor metrics and discussion for at least two code completion vendors, specifically Tabnine and Amazon CodeWhisperer (and also GitHub Copilot), including latency and acceptance-rate figures.

citation-quality.requirement: Grounds timing recommendations in cited research rather than personal anecdotes or team experiences
citation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly bases timing guidance on purported empirical studies and field metrics and provides a long list of citations to support latency thresholds and acceptance-rate claims, rather than relying on personal anecdotes or team experiences.

citation-quality.requirement: Grounds timing recommendations solely in personal blogs, forums, or social media without citing academic papers or official vendor research
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites a mix of academic/HCI sources (e.g., Card/Moran/Newell, Miller, arXiv papers) and official vendor documentation/research (GitHub, AWS, JetBrains, Tabnine), so its timing recommendations are not grounded solely in personal blogs, forums, or social media.
